hook.text: Fall into a small black hole and gravity will stretch you into a thin strand of atoms. Physicists call this spaghettification.

buildup.text: Near a black hole, gravity is much stronger at your feet than at your head—a tidal force.

discovery.text: This difference stretches you vertically and compresses you horizontally. You become like spaghetti.

twist.text: For supermassive black holes, tidal forces at the horizon are gentle. You'd cross without noticing.

climax.text: In 2020, astronomers observed a star being spaghettified by a black hole 215 million light-years away.

punchline.text: Stretched like pasta by gravity. A real physics term. A real bad way to go.

quiz.question: Why would you survive crossing the event horizon of a supermassive black hole but not a small one?
options: The tidal forces at a supermassive black hole's horizon are much weaker
Supermassive black holes have no event horizon
Light still escapes from supermassive black holes
